PARAGRAPH 1
To solve this historical data filtering problem we must create a robust python function that efficiently processes large lists of date strings while adhering to strict logical rules regarding century definitions. The core challenge involves correctly mapping a given integer century to its specific range of years, because the mathematical relationship between centuries and years is not always intuitive to casual observers. A common mistake is assuming the nineteenth century begins in nineteen hundred, yet historically it actually spans from eighteen hundred one through nineteen hundred. Our solution must therefore calculate the starting year as the century number multiplied by one hundred minus ninety nine, and the ending year as the century number multiplied by one hundred. This precise calculation ensures that dates like eighteen hundred one are included while nineteen hundred one is correctly excluded for the nineteenth century. We will implement this logic using simple integer arithmetic to maintain high performance even when processing millions of records in a large dataset. The function will accept a list of strings formatted as year dash month dash day along with an integer representing the target century. Inside the function we will iterate through each date string and extract the year component by splitting the string at the first dash character. This approach avoids the overhead of importing heavy datetime libraries for simple string parsing tasks which can significantly slow down execution on massive datasets. Once the year is extracted as an integer we compare it against our calculated start and end boundaries to determine if it belongs to the specified century. If the year falls within the inclusive range we append the original date string to our result list. This method preserves the original formatting of the input data which is crucial for downstream analysis or reporting tasks. We must also consider edge cases such as empty input lists or invalid date formats that might cause the function to crash if not handled gracefully. Although the prompt specifies valid input formats a production ready function should ideally include error handling to skip malformed entries without stopping the entire process. By using list comprehension we can write the filtering logic in a single concise line that is both readable and fast. The efficiency of this approach comes from avoiding unnecessary object creation and relying on native string operations which are highly optimized in modern python implementations. Researchers dealing with historical archives will find this tool invaluable for quickly isolating events from specific eras without manual sorting or filtering. The logic remains consistent regardless of the century requested whether it is the first century or the twenty fifth.
